scenarios.task: We installed a highly visible new turnstile and a large 'Protected by 24/7 Security' sign at the main entrance. Employees and visitors feel much more reassured, and it's become a bit of a talking point for how seriously we take security. I think this was a strong investment.
criteria: Questions whether the visible control actually raises attacker cost or improves detection, rather than accepting reassurance as evidence of effectiveness
Names this explicitly as a security theater risk if the control doesn't meaningfully contribute to deterrence, detection, delay, or response
Recommends evaluating the specific control against the four functions (deterrence, detection, delay, response) instead of visibility/reassurance alone
States that a control that looks strong but doesn't function well against the actual threat model can create false confidence

scenarios.task: Our data center's security relies on one very strong control: a single high-security mantrap door with biometric access at the main entrance. It's an excellent, expensive system and I'm confident it makes the facility secure.
criteria: Flags relying on one strong control as single-layer reliance, naming the failure mode explicitly regardless of how good that one control is
States that a determined, resourced adversary will eventually defeat any specific control, and one strong layer is still a single point of failure
Recommends layered, redundant controls (perimeter, monitoring, response capability) rather than concentrating protection in one mechanism
Doesn't dismiss the mantrap's value — the issue is the absence of independent additional layers, not that this control is bad

scenarios.task: We've invested heavily in perimeter security — cameras, badge access, fencing — and I feel good about our coverage. We don't currently have a process for periodically reviewing who still has legitimate access or watching for concerning behavior from existing employees, since our controls are focused on keeping unauthorized people out.
criteria: Flags the absence of insider-threat measures as a real gap, naming the ignoring-the-insider-threat failure mode
States that perimeter-focused security systematically underweights risk from people who already have legitimate access
Recommends periodic access-rights review and behavioral awareness as a distinct, necessary category perimeter controls don't address
Notes this isn't an argument against the perimeter investment, but that it addresses a different threat category than insider risk

scenarios.task: We have a detailed, well-written incident response plan for security incidents — clear roles, escalation paths, contact lists. It's been sitting finalized for over a year, and we haven't run a drill or tabletop exercise against it since it was written, since the document itself is thorough and we're confident it covers what's needed.
criteria: Flags the lack of any drill or tabletop exercise as a real gap, naming the untested-response-plans failure mode regardless of the document's thoroughness
States that a plan's actual effectiveness under real time pressure is unknown until it's been tested
Recommends running a drill or tabletop exercise to surface gaps now, rather than discovering them during an actual incident
Distinguishes a well-written plan on paper from a proven, rehearsed one
